scenario: You find a lost wallet with cash and an ID card inside. What do you do?

options.text: Take the cash and return the wallet
Leave it where it is and walk away

scenario: A close friend asks you to lie to their partner to cover for them. What do you do?

options.text: Refuse and encourage them to be honest
Avoid answering and change the subject

scenario: At work you notice a colleague taking small office supplies home regularly. What do you do?

options.text: Talk to them privately about it

scenario: You discover an error in your favor on an invoice (you were undercharged). What do you do?

options.text: Notify the company and pay the difference
Say nothing and keep the benefit
Wait to see if they notice first

scenario: You can help a stranger with directions, but you're already late. What do you do?

options.text: Give a quick answer and leave
Pretend you didn't hear and move on

scenario: Your team can meet a deadline only by skipping some testing. What do you do?

options.text: Insist on testing even if the deadline slips
Skip testing to hit the deadline
Suggest a smaller scope to keep both quality and time
Let the manager decide and follow along

scenario: You hear a harmful rumor about someone. Sharing it could make you fit in. What do you do?

options.text: Do not share it and discourage others from spreading it
Share it with a few people you trust
Ask where it came from and verify before reacting

scenario: You see someone shoplifting basic food items. What do you do?

options.text: Do not report, but reflect on why it might be happening
Offer to pay for the items if possible

scenario: A teacher gives you points by mistake that improve your grade. What do you do?

options.text: Tell the teacher and accept the corrected grade
Keep quiet and accept the better grade
Hint at the mistake without insisting

scenario: You can choose between a cheaper product with questionable labor practices and a more expensive ethical alternative. What do you do?

options.text: Buy the ethical alternative even if it costs more
Buy the cheaper option because budget matters
Research more and delay the purchase

scenario: A runaway trolley is heading toward five people on the track. You can pull a lever to divert it to another track where it will hit one person. What do you do?

options.text: Pull the lever to save five

scenario: In a variant of the trolley problem, you could stop the trolley by pushing a very large man off a bridge, which would kill him but save five. What do you do?

options.text: Look for another way even if uncertain

scenario: A person you care about needs a lifesaving medicine they cannot afford. You could steal it from a pharmacy. What do you do?

options.text: Do not steal; seek legal alternatives

scenario: You and several others are in an overcrowded lifeboat during a storm. If nobody leaves, everyone may die. What do you do?

options.text: Support a lottery to decide who leaves

scenario: A prosecutor offers you a plea deal: if you testify against someone you believe is innocent, you’ll avoid jail. What do you do?

options.text: Refuse to lie, even if it costs you
Ask for more time and legal advice

scenario: You witness a minor car accident. The other driver offers you money to say you didn't see anything. What do you do?

options.text: Refuse and give an honest statement
Accept the money and stay silent

scenario: You find a phone with emergency contacts visible. The owner looks panicked nearby. What do you do?

options.text: Wait to see if someone claims it

scenario: A coworker confides they are struggling and missing deadlines. Your manager asks why performance is slipping. What do you do?

options.text: Share only what is necessary and protect privacy
Disclose everything to protect the team

scenario: You accidentally receive a confidential email not meant for you. What do you do?

options.text: Notify the sender and delete it

scenario: A charity fundraiser exaggerates statistics to get donations for a good cause. Do you support the tactic?

options.text: No—truth matters even for good causes
Yes—helping more people is worth it
Support the cause but insist on accurate messaging

scenario: A friend asks for your honest opinion about their work, but you know it will hurt them. What do you do?

options.text: Praise it to protect their feelings
Give vague feedback and avoid specifics

scenario: You see someone being verbally harassed on public transport. What do you do?
A teammate takes credit for your work in a meeting. What do you do?

options.text: Let it go to keep peace

scenario: You can report a safety issue that will delay a project and upset leadership. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore it unless someone gets hurt

scenario: Your neighbor plays loud music late every night. What do you do?
You discover your company collects more user data than needed. What do you do?

options.text: Raise the issue and push for minimization

scenario: A social media post about someone is going viral with false info. You can correct it but may get attacked. What do you do?
You can get a friend a job by recommending them, but they are not qualified. What do you do?

options.text: Recommend them only for a role they can do

scenario: A restaurant gives you the wrong order, but it’s better and they didn’t notice. What do you do?

options.text: Ask if you can keep it and pay the difference

scenario: You see a lost child crying in a store. What do you do?

options.text: Ask someone else to handle it

scenario: You could donate a small amount that won’t affect you, but you’re not obligated. What do you do?

options.text: Donate only if friends are watching

scenario: You find a bug in an online store that lets you get expensive items for free. What do you do?
You overhear a friend planning to cheat on an exam. What do you do?
You can save money by using software without paying for it. What do you do?

options.text: Pay or use a free alternative

scenario: You witness a friend being rude to service staff. What do you do?

options.text: Call it out and apologize to staff

scenario: A homeless person asks for money. You’re unsure if it helps. What do you do?

options.text: Decline and donate to a shelter later

scenario: You can return an item you used once, even though it’s against the store’s policy. What do you do?

options.text: Return it and claim it’s unused

scenario: A coworker asks you to cover their shift, but you need rest. What do you do?

options.text: Say no and set a boundary

scenario: You find out a small local business underpays staff. You like their products. What do you do?

options.text: Stop supporting them and tell others
Keep buying; it’s not your responsibility
Talk to the owner or staff first

scenario: A family member asks you to keep a secret that could harm them later. What do you do?
You’re offered a bribe to speed up paperwork. What do you do?
A friend posts a photo of you online that you dislike. What do you do?
You can get a tax benefit by stretching the truth slightly. What do you do?

options.text: Ask an accountant for a legal approach

scenario: You notice someone left their credit card at the checkout. What do you do?

options.text: Hold it and wait for them to return

scenario: You can reduce your commute emissions by biking, but it’s inconvenient. What do you do?
A classmate asks to copy your homework. What do you do?
You can tip extra to help a struggling waiter, but it’s not required. What do you do?
You witness bullying at school/work. Reporting may make you a target. What do you do?

options.text: Report it and support the victim

scenario: You promised to attend a friend’s event, but you’re exhausted and need rest. What do you do?

options.text: Go anyway to keep your promise

scenario: You can help a coworker succeed, but it might reduce your own recognition. What do you do?

options.text: Help only if it benefits you too

scenario: A public official you support is caught in a scandal. The evidence is strong. What do you do?

options.text: Defend them to protect your side

scenario: You discover a friend’s partner is cheating. Your friend doesn’t know. What do you do?
A parent asks you to keep a secret from the other parent. You are a child in this scenario. What do you do?

options.text: Ask the adult to resolve it themselves

scenario: You’re a hiring manager and a candidate reveals they need accommodations. It may slow the team. What do you do?

options.text: Hire based on merit and provide accommodations
Hire them only if budget allows

scenario: You can choose to pay a little more for sustainable packaging. What do you do?
You receive too much change in cash. What do you do?
A teammate is new and slowing the project down. What do you do?
You can break a rule to help someone in a crisis. What do you do?

options.text: Find a workaround within the rules

scenario: You find a colleague’s resume on a printer and see they’re applying elsewhere. What do you do?

options.text: Ask them if they want to talk

scenario: You can vote for a policy that benefits you but harms a minority group. What do you do?
A colleague makes an offensive joke. Laughing would make you fit in. What do you do?
You have evidence that a friend is driving drunk tonight. What do you do?

options.text: Stop them and call a ride
Warn them but let them decide

scenario: A hospital has one ventilator and two patients who need it. You’re on the ethics committee. What do you do?

options.text: Give it to the patient with best prognosis

scenario: You find a journal on a bench. It contains private thoughts. What do you do?

options.text: Try to return it without reading
Read a little to identify the owner

scenario: You can pay to skip a long queue. Others cannot afford it. What do you do?

options.text: Do not skip; it feels unfair

scenario: A friend shares a secret about someone else with you. It could damage reputations. What do you do?

options.text: Encourage your friend to handle it responsibly

scenario: You can get ahead by taking a shortcut that makes life harder for a coworker. What do you do?

options.text: Take it but offer to help them later

scenario: You are moderating an online community. A post is rude but not hateful. What do you do?

options.text: Remove it to protect the tone
Leave it and encourage better behavior

scenario: A company offers you a refund but you know you weren’t actually eligible. What do you do?
You’re asked to sign an NDA that hides a harmful product defect. What do you do?

options.text: Sign it but plan to whistleblow later

scenario: A friend is raising money for a cause you disagree with. They ask you to donate. What do you do?

options.text: Try to persuade them to change causes

scenario: You find evidence that your favorite influencer fakes charity work. What do you do?

options.text: Stop supporting and share the evidence
Ignore it because you like them

scenario: A new law is unfair but legal. You can exploit it for profit. What do you do?

options.text: Exploit it while advocating to change it

scenario: A friend asks for a loan. You can afford it but might not get it back. What do you do?

options.text: Give a smaller amount as a gift

scenario: You have to choose between attending a family event and a work commitment you already accepted. What do you do?
You can save a lot of time by using AI to write an assignment, but it’s against the rules. What do you do?

options.text: Use AI only for brainstorming within allowed rules

scenario: A friend asks you to share your streaming password. It’s against terms of service. What do you do?

options.text: Offer to split the cost legally

scenario: You can report a minor fraud, but it will likely cost someone their job. What do you do?

options.text: Talk to them first to correct it

scenario: A team vote is split. You can sway it by exaggerating risks. What do you do?
Your child asks if Santa is real. What do you do?

options.text: Ask what they think and respond

scenario: A coworker is being discriminated against subtly. Speaking up could hurt your career. What do you do?
You are late and can cut in line without being noticed. What do you do?

options.text: Ask politely if you can go ahead

scenario: A friend wants you to keep their location secret from their family. You suspect they may be unsafe. What do you do?

options.text: Keep it but check on their safety

scenario: You can donate blood, but you dislike needles. What do you do?

options.text: Look for another way to help

scenario: Your friend’s startup asks you to invest. You doubt it will succeed. What do you do?
You see someone drop cash. They don’t notice and walk away. What do you do?
A friend asks you to be a reference, but you think they’d be a bad hire. What do you do?
You can avoid paying for public transport by sneaking in. What do you do?
A person asks you to sign a petition you don’t fully understand. What do you do?
You can report a neighbor’s small illegal rental to authorities, which could remove housing for a family. What do you do?
You learn your friend is being emotionally abused. They ask you not to tell anyone. What do you do?

options.text: Respect privacy but encourage help and safety planning
Tell someone who can help immediately
Do nothing to honor their request

scenario: You are offered a promotion that requires relocating and would separate you from family support. What do you do?
You can choose to share a colleague’s mistake to protect yourself. What do you do?

options.text: Own your part and do not scapegoat

scenario: A friend asks you to keep their illegal side hustle secret. You think it’s low harm. What do you do?

options.text: Tell them to stop and warn them

scenario: You are asked to host a guest you dislike for a night. It would help them. What do you do?
You can volunteer for a community cleanup, but you’d rather rest. What do you do?
You find out your company’s marketing is misleading but not technically false. What do you do?
A friend sends you a private screenshot of someone’s messages. They ask your opinion. What do you do?

options.text: Tell them this is not okay

scenario: You can get a refund by claiming a package never arrived, but it did. What do you do?

options.text: Request a discount for inconvenience only if true

scenario: A team member is sick but keeps working. You worry about burnout. What do you do?
You see a dog left in a hot car. The owner is not around. What do you do?

options.text: Call authorities and try to help
Break the window to save the dog
Wait and hope the owner returns

scenario: You can make money by promoting a product you know is low quality. What do you do?
A friend is angry and asks you to take their side in a conflict you don’t fully understand. What do you do?
You can choose a cheaper flight with higher emissions or a train that is slower but greener. What do you do?

options.text: Choose based on necessity each time

scenario: A friend asks you to keep them company while they confront someone. It’s stressful for you. What do you do?
You are asked to review a colleague’s work. You notice a small error that could hurt them later. What do you do?
A neighbor’s package is delivered to your door. What do you do?

options.text: Leave it outside and ignore it

scenario: You can choose to buy from a small local shop or a cheaper big corporation. What do you do?
You are offered inside information in a business deal. Using it is unethical but profitable. What do you do?
Someone asks you to translate a message that contains a hidden insult. What do you do?
You can adopt a pet, but you may not have time to care for it properly. What do you do?
A friend asks to borrow your car. You worry they might drive recklessly. What do you do?
You see someone take the last accessible parking spot without needing it. What do you do?
A friend wants to vent by insulting someone else. It makes you uncomfortable. What do you do?
You can choose to buy a used item or a new one. The new one is nicer. What do you do?
You are asked to keep a workplace secret that hides harassment. What do you do?

options.text: Keep quiet to protect your job
Support the victim and encourage reporting

scenario: A friend invites you to a gathering you know will exclude someone unfairly. What do you do?

options.text: Try to include the excluded person

scenario: You can choose to correct a stranger’s misinformation in public, but it might embarrass them. What do you do?
You have a chance to return to a store to pay for something you accidentally walked out with. What do you do?

options.text: Pay only if you return anyway

scenario: You notice a friend’s relationship is unhealthy, but they didn’t ask for advice. What do you do?

options.text: Check in and express concern gently

scenario: You can choose to share your notes with classmates, but you worked hard and worry others will benefit without effort. What do you do?
You’re asked to vote on layoffs. Keeping profits high means layoffs; reducing profits means keeping jobs. What do you do?

options.text: Protect jobs even if profits fall
Seek alternatives like pay cuts or reduced hours

scenario: A friend reveals they committed a serious crime in the past and feel guilty. What do you do?

options.text: Encourage them to seek legal/ethical accountability
Keep it secret to protect them

scenario: You can choose to correct a billing error that benefits your employer but harms customers. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore it because it benefits the company

scenario: A friend asks you to help them stalk an ex online. What do you do?
You can choose to accept a gift from a vendor, which might bias your decisions at work. What do you do?
You witness a friend stealing from a large corporation. They say it doesn’t matter. What do you do?

options.text: Tell them to stop and return it

scenario: You can choose to take a job that pays well but conflicts with your values. What do you do?

options.text: Take it temporarily while seeking alternatives

scenario: You are offered a chance to speak publicly about an issue, but you fear backlash. What do you do?
You can choose to spend time with a lonely relative or pursue a personal hobby. What do you do?
A friend asks you to lie on their behalf to get a refund. What do you do?

options.text: Offer to help in a legal way

scenario: You find a notebook containing someone’s business idea. You could use it for yourself. What do you do?
You’re in a group project. One person does nothing. What do you do?

options.text: Address it directly and redistribute tasks

scenario: You can choose to give honest feedback that may cost someone a scholarship/job. What do you do?
You see a stranger collapse. You’re not sure what to do. What do you do?
You can choose to support a policy that increases surveillance to reduce crime. What do you do?
A friend tells you they might harm themselves. They beg you not to tell anyone. What do you do?

options.text: Keep it secret and just support them

scenario: You can choose to vote for a candidate you dislike, but who supports policies that help many people. What do you do?
You are asked to keep a corporate secret that hides environmental harm. What do you do?

options.text: Report internally and push to stop harm

scenario: You can choose to repair something old or replace it with a new one. Repair is harder. What do you do?
You are a judge in a game show. Cheating is obvious, but the contestant is in desperate need. What do you do?
You find a lost USB drive labeled 'private'. What do you do?
You can choose to stop paying attention to news to protect your mental health, but you worry about becoming uninformed. What do you do?
You are asked to coach someone who previously treated you poorly. What do you do?
You can choose to share your location with a partner for safety, but it reduces privacy. What do you do?
A friend asks you to join a boycott. You agree with the goal but it’s inconvenient. What do you do?
You can choose to pay extra to offset carbon emissions for a trip. What do you do?
You are a bystander when a cashier is being yelled at. What do you do?
You can choose to share a friend’s good news before they announce it. What do you do?

options.text: Do not share; it’s their story

scenario: You can choose to spend your free evening helping someone move or relaxing at home. What do you do?

options.text: Offer partial help or another time
